items.q: What is the dramatic function of a soliloquy?
items.correct: It reveals a character's inner thoughts directly to the audience while the character is alone on stage — a convention granting access to private psychology.
items.misconception: A soliloquy is any long speech a character gives.
items.random_wrong: A soliloquy is when characters argue with each other.
items.why: Students confuse soliloquy (alone, inner thoughts) with monologue (long speech, possibly to others). The key feature is solitude and direct access to thought — losing this loses the convention's force.

items.correct: The claim that judging a text by what the author 'intended' is fallacious — the text's meaning lies in the work itself, not the author's stated or guessed intentions.
items.misconception: It means authors can never make mistakes when writing.
items.random_wrong: It means it is wrong to write fiction.
items.why: Students assume 'author intent' settles meaning. Wimsatt and Beardsley argued it doesn't — meaning is a public property of the text, not a private fact about the author.

items.q: What did Romantic poets value in nature?
items.correct: Nature as a source of spiritual insight and emotional truth, accessed through imagination — not picturesque scenery for its own sake.
items.misconception: Romantic poets just liked to write about pretty landscapes.
items.random_wrong: Romantic poets believed nature was full of fairies and elves.
items.why: The Romantic engagement with nature was philosophical — it was a route to truth, the sublime and the self. Reducing it to 'pretty scenery' misses Wordsworth's epistemology entirely.
Correct answer: Brecht. “Beckett” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Who wrote A Streetcar Named Desire?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Williams. “Miller” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Waiting for Godot first performed?
items.why: The real figure is 1953 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
